rationale_for_baseline_tier: A platelet count of ~75 with no mucocutaneous bleeding and a benign tension-headache pattern carries very low bleeding risk; the correct course is accurate reassurance, treating the tension headache, and routine GP/haematology follow-up — a T1 baseline. A firm bleeding/neurological safety-net is mandatory (counts can change; ICH, though rare, is catastrophic), and emergency escalation now would be over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New petechiae/lots of bruising, or bleeding that won't stop (nose, gums, urine, stool)
escalation_edges.rationale: Would indicate a falling count/wet purpura with higher bleeding risk.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get lots of new tiny red spots or bruises, or bleeding that won't stop from your nose, gums, urine or stool, go to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden 'worst ever' explosive headache, one-sided weakness/facial droop, slurred speech, or vomiting
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a sudden worst-ever explosive headache, weakness or drooping on one side, slurred speech, or vomiting, call 000 immediately.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; maintain haematology monitoring; know the bleeding/neurology red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: A platelet count of about 75 is a safe level and you have no bleeding signs, so you are not bleeding into your brain — your headache is a tension headache. Use paracetamol (not aspirin/anti-inflammatories), do slow breathing, and log off the results portal. See your GP within a week to repeat the count. Call 000 for a sudden worst-ever headache, one-sided weakness, or slurred speech, and go to ED for lots of new bruises/red spots or bleeding that won't stop.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP within 1 week; 000/ED for red flags
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Only attend ED overnight for a bleeding or neurological red flag; otherwise this can wait for the GP.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth review of a panic attack with tension headache over a mildly low platelet count (~75, safe range); no mucocutaneous bleeding or focal neurology; reassurance and paracetamol advised (aspirin/NSAIDs avoided); bleeding/neurological safety-net and routine haematology follow-up recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing as anxiety with no bleeding/neurology screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A genuinely falling count with wet purpura, or an actual ICH, is missed because red flags were never screened.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/ED for a safe count with no bleeding/neurology is over-triage and reinforces catastrophic beliefs.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Cannot obtain a current platelet count or perform a formal neurological exam/imaging remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Reassure using the known safe count (~75) and a mucocutaneous/neurology screen, with a firm bleeding/neurological safety-net.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismiss without screening or dispatch an ambulance for a safe count.
